params.text:
Knowing the person and using the care plan

You cannot deliver person-centred care unless you truly know the person. That means understanding their history, preferences, wishes and needs — their life story, their family and culture, their routines, and the things that make them feel themselves.

The care and support plan is your main tool. It is a living document, owned by the individual and shared with others only with their consent. It should:

  • Reflect the person’s physical, mental, emotional, social, cultural and spiritual needs.
  • Record their preferences and the outcomes that matter to them.
  • Be reviewed and updated regularly, because needs change over time.

Always read and follow the current plan, and report anything that has changed so it can be kept accurate.

params.alt: A carer listening to the person they support

params.question: You notice that Mr Ahmed no longer manages to eat with the cutlery described in his care plan, and often leaves meals unfinished. What is the most person-centred response?

answers.text: Report the change so his care plan can be reviewed and adapted to his current needs.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Care plans must be kept up to date as needs change, and changes should be reported and reviewed.

answers.text: Keep following the plan exactly as written, because it was signed off.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A care plan is a living document. Following an out-of-date plan can leave needs unmet.

answers.text: Start feeding him yourself at every meal to save time.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This removes his independence without exploring what he can still do or prefers. Independence should be supported, not taken away.

answers.text: Assume he is simply not hungry and record nothing.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Assumptions are not person-centred. A change in eating should be noticed, explored and reported.
